title: Emergent conserved quantities via irreversibility
authors: Alex Blokhuis, Martijn van Kuppeveld, Daan van de Weem, Robert Pollice
abstract: Conserved quantities increasingly underpin the inference of physical models. Recently new conserved quantities have been found in this context, that currently lack an interpretation. Here, we show that irreversible reactions in CRNs and Markov Chains lead to emergent conservation laws and broken cycles. Linearly dependent currents - characterized by the "co-production index" - arise due to irreversible reactions. We derive a law relating conserved quantities, broken cycles, and co-production. This resolves a recent conundrum posed by a machine-discovered candidate for a non-integer conservation law. Our findings introduce heretofore overlooked extensions to a widely used index law for CRNs and Markov Chains that undercounts conservation laws. This furnishes new tools and immediate applications for the inference and analysis of models based on conservation laws.
summary: The paper identifies a mechanism where irreversible reactions create emergent conservation laws that standard structural analysis misses. By introducing the co-production index, the authors provide a theoretical basis for previously unexplained 'anomalous' quantities in chemical and stochastic models.
main_problem: The difficulty in predicting the total number of observable conserved quantities in complex chemical reaction networks (CRNs) and Markov chains, specifically addressing why standard structural laws undercount them.
main_result: The discovery of a 'co-production law' where irreversible reactions and collinear currents lead to emergent, hidden conservation laws and broken cycles, resolving previously unexplained non-integer conservation laws found via machine learning.
method: A mathematical framework involving a 'reaction merging' procedure for proportional currents, the definition of a 'co-production index', and the use of fundamental subspaces of the stoichiometric matrix.
model_or_system: The theory is applied to Chemical Reaction Networks (CRNs), Markov chains, and specifically to $n$-mer Random Sequential Adsorption (RSA) on a discrete lattice and atmospheric chemistry models.
key_observables: Conserved quantities (left nullvectors of the stoichiometric matrix), reaction currents, co-production index, and gap populations in RSA.
important_parameters: Co-production index (Upsilon), number of broken cycles (Lambda), and reaction rate constants.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes mass-action kinetics for CRNs and first-order transitions for the stochastic-to-deterministic link; it also assumes polynomial flow for numerical verification.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows an atmospheric CRN with collinear and cycle reactions; Figure 2 illustrates trimer adsorption steps and lattice configurations; Figure 3 depicts the reaction merging procedure; Figure 4 reconstructs the atmospheric model; Figure 5 shows the convergence of ensemble averages.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of undercounted conservation laws, proposes the reaction merging technique and the co-production law, validates the theory using atmospheric chemistry and RSA models, and extends the framework to stochastic master equations and non-mass-action kinetics.
why_interesting: This work provides a new way to identify hidden symmetries and invariants in non-equilibrium systems, which is directly applicable to understanding dissipation and emergent constraints in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: An Atmospheric CRN model[37, 38] with a recent machine-discovered candidate conserved quantity (CQ3 in Ref.[29]). The co-production index Υ = 1 is given next to its two collinear reactions (also highlighted in pink). These do not form a cycle. The cycle index c = 1 is given next to its three cycle reactions (highlighted in blue). Thus, no cycles break upon merging collinear reactions: ∆c = 0, ∆ℓ= +1, i.e., a co-production conservation law then results from the co-production law Υ = ∆ℓ−∆c, confirming the numerically found CQ3 marks the detection of a genuine conservation law.

FIG. 3: Illustration of the general merging procedure. a) Representation with each reaction represented once (S). Reactions with pairwise proportional partners are highlighted in fuchsia. b) Representation after merging pairwise proportional reactions (S▷▷). Υ = 4 reactions were merged with respect to S. This results in one em- anant and three broken cycles.

FIG. 4: Figure 1 from the main text, reproduced for convenience. An Atmospheric CRN model with co- production index Υ = 1. Collinear reactions are high- lighted in pink. These do not include cycle reactions - highlighted in blue - and, hence, no cycles break upon merging collinear reactions ∧= 0.

FIG. 5: a) Evaluation of conserved quantities over av- erages after 1 reaction, by averaging over an increasing number of simulations. As the number of simulations increases, convergence occurs to the theoretical values (dotted line) of L(1), L(2), L(3). b) Averaged number of gaps [0], [1], [2] as a function of the number of individual simulated realizations, for trimer (n = 3) adsorption on a lattice of size L = 9. Dotted lines correspond to theoretical expectation values L(1), L(2), L(3), to which the simulated averages converge.
